steps.title: Identify the noun and its identifying title
steps.content: The left side of the blank contains the noun "song" (in "the hit song"). The right side begins with a title in quotation marks, "Little Marie," that directly identifies the song.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for titles
steps.content: Rule: when a noun is immediately followed by a quoted title that identifies it, no punctuation separates them. The noun is "song" and the title is "Little Marie." Reconstructed sentence with each choice: A) "the hit song \"Little Marie\"" — noun flows directly into its title with no punctuation; correct. B) "the hit song, \"Little Marie\"" — the comma incorrectly sets off the title as if it were a non-essential appositive, but a title identifying a specific song is essential; eliminated. C) "the hit song: \"Little Marie\"" — a colon is used to introduce an elaboration after a complete clause; "the hit song" is not a complete clause; eliminated. D) "the hit song; \"Little Marie\"" — a semicolon joins two independent clauses; "the hit song" is not a clause; eliminated. Verdict: A is correct.
